text: young male patient presented to a hospital with a complaint of right hypochondriac pain intermittent which was and with jaundice for seven to ten days wvc counts were eighteen thousand and bilbin was ten point fifty nine and he had raised liver enzymes ultrasound suggested a mirage syndrome ct scan was done and that showed a conglomerated collection on the valvular fossa with the intrahepatic gb with the dilated cbd
MRI with MRCP was done to confirm the findings which suggested almost the same findings In view of current finding in cholangitis ERCP was done and stent was placed
Upon opening the abdomen, dense adhesions are visible between the first part of the duodenum and the inferior surface of the gallbladder.
Upon initial inspection, the gallbladder is not visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon observes natural adhesions between the colon and the right lobe of the liver, indicating dense tissue connections in this area.

The surgeon uses monopolar diathermy to remove adhesions, performing careful dissection flush to the liver surface. The duodenum is meticulously separated from the underlying bile duct and the inferior surface of the liver.
The surgeon observes a dilated bile duct with several lymph nodes visible surrounding it.
The surgeon separates the duodenum from the bile duct, revealing the distinct anatomical structures as the dissection progresses.
The surgeon identifies and establishes a dissection plane, likely between the gallbladder and surrounding structures, as part of the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon continues the dissection, gradually separating the duodenum from the dense tissue surrounding the bile duct, using careful technique to maintain clear anatomical planes.
The surgical field shows the absence of the gallbladder, with the surrounding anatomical structures visible but no gallbladder present.
Probably it was a Mereza syndrome.
So maybe it was a bile duct or maybe it was a Hartman's pouch which was directly connected to CBD.
The surgeon explores the bile duct after securing the anatomical structures, focusing on the area where the gallbladder was previously not visible.
The surgeon uses diathermy to puncture the bile duct, revealing bile as the duct is opened after confirming the presence of a stone.
The surgeon observes an endobiliary stent previously placed within the bile duct, alongside a large stone situated near the stent's density.
The surgeon extracts a large stone from the bile duct, which was previously identified near the endobiliary stent.
The bile duct is visible with an endobiliary stent in place, surrounded by surrounding tissue.
The surgeon observes a single stone and the opening of the gallbladder, indicating a potential Mirizzi syndrome on the right side.
To confirm the presence of stone which was there probably in intrahepatic location,
we decided to go for IOC intraoperative cholangiogram

An infant feeding tube is inserted into the suspected area on the right side, and contrast is injected. The video shows a filling defect, with the entire right biliary system becoming visible, suggesting a possible facialis communication.
The video shows the stone located at the top of the bile duct, which is communicating with the right side of the duct.
The surgeon explores the area and performs a vertical incision on the interior wall of the collection, possibly a gallbladder or hepatotomy, to open the collection.
Using monocular diathermy, the surgeon opens a cavity revealing granulation tissue and a large stone inside. The stone appears to have migrated into the liver, eroding the right hepatic ducts.
The surgeon extracts a large stone from the cavity and places it in Morrison's pouch. After washing and lavaging the cavity, the clear opening of the right hepatic duct becomes visible.
So given the current situation, we decided to stent the system and close the system rather than going for radical surgery.

so the previously placed stent which was there were going on the segment four which was now placed and parked in the right hepatic ductal system and the position of the stent was secured now and it was going in the right sided system and another endobiliary stent was capped surgically on the
The surgeon plans to close the defect in the biliary tree, positioning two endobiliary stents to the left side of the ductal system.
The surgeon uses polygalactic 2-0 suture material on a round-bodied needle to close the defect with multiple figure-of-eight sutures, addressing the large and dilated area.
The surgeon carefully sutures the common bile duct (CBD) from top to bottom, ensuring there is no stricture or narrowing of the bile duct as the biliary tree is closed.
The surgeon sutures the Hartmann's pouch or biliary area, followed by suturing the collection cavity or hepatotomy to minimize potential biliary leakage from the defect area.
The surgeon closes the defect with several interrupted sutures, aiming to facilitate early healing of the cavity and potentially reduce biliary drain output.
The surgeon uses multiple sutures to obliterate and close the defect in the biliary tree, ensuring the area is securely sealed.

After closing the defect, the surgical field is irrigated with saline. A small amount of bile drainage is observed from the suture site. Gallstones are placed in a specimen retrieval bag. A flat drain is positioned in Morrison's pouch, and the omentum is draped over it, marking the conclusion of the surgery.

and the patient recovered well following the surgery was discharged in post of day three rain was out by day seven without any significant biliary leakage stent should remove six weeks following the surgery and fallout patient is doing well without any complaints
